title: Sci-Fi Gear & Equipment
description: Tools, weapons, devices, and curiosities found in a space-faring setting — from standard-issue equipment to salvaged oddities and black-market hardware.

entries.result: A plasma cutter modified for use as a sidearm. The modification is illegal in most jurisdictions, but the cuts are cleaner.
A survival kit packed for a standard desert world — useless on the current planet, but complete and sealed.
A personal translation module that handles forty-two languages and misidentifies three of them in ways that have caused incidents.
A grav-anchor — a small device that pins the user to a surface in zero-g. The release mechanism sticks.
A data spike: a narrow tool for injecting code into physical terminals. Used, and not wiped.
A ration pack rated for fourteen days. The nutritional content is accurate. The flavor is not worth discussing.
A long-range comm unit with a range of two light-minutes. The encryption is three generations out of date.
A void suit patch kit. The patches are rated for six hours in hard vacuum. Four of the twelve patches have been used.
A personal shield generator — bracelet-sized — that projects a half-second barrier against energy weapons. Recharge time is forty seconds.
A scanner that reads atmospheric composition, temperature, and basic biological markers. The biological sensor is miscalibrated.
A compact medical kit with surgical-grade tools and a diagnostic wand. The drugs are expired by eight months.
A handheld gravity meter that can identify anomalies in local gravity fields. Has an alarm set to a threshold no one has documented.
A torch that burns at three thousand degrees. Intended for mining. The safety grip is cracked.
A wrist-mounted grappling unit with thirty meters of high-tensile monofilament. The motor sometimes fires in reverse.
A scout drone, palm-sized, with a range of five kilometers and a two-hour battery. The camera cuts out at irregular intervals.
A pair of mag-boots. Standard crew issue. One boot's field is weaker than the other, causing a slight lean.
A thermal cloak rated for extreme cold. Blocks heat signatures as a side effect, which some users find useful.
A portable airlock bonder — a tool for attaching temporary seals between hull sections. Has one seal remaining.
A neural interface headset for operating drones or vehicles remotely. Leaves users disoriented for several minutes after use.
A stun baton that delivers a paralyzing charge. The charge setting is stuck at maximum.
A light-bending panel — a square meter of material that deflects visual light, creating a partial optical blind. Doesn't work on infrared.
A chemical analyzer that can identify compounds from a small sample. The readout is in a notation system no one on the crew recognizes.
A compressed-air pistol loaded with tranquilizer rounds rated for large fauna. Effective at twelve meters.
A deployable perimeter sensor array — six stakes and a hub — that detects movement within fifty meters and reports via comm.
A personal locator beacon that transmits to rescue services. The transmission has been running for eleven hours.
A folded emergency shelter rated for four occupants. It deploys in three seconds and takes twenty minutes to repack.
A short-range jammer that blocks comm traffic within a twenty-meter radius. Also blocks the user's own comm.
A precision laser designator used to mark targets for orbital or air support. Requires a receiver on the other end to be useful.
A handheld fabricator that can produce simple objects from raw feedstock. Feedstock is not included and is expensive.
A military-grade armored gauntlet, single. The rest of the armor set is not present.
A sealed sample container rated for biohazard level three. The seal is intact. Something is inside.
A programmable restraint — a cuff that can be configured to allow or deny movement based on user-set biometric triggers.
A mining charge rated for three meters of rock. Unstable above forty degrees Celsius.
A jump-wire kit for bypassing electronic locks. Works on older systems. Newer systems detect it and trigger an alarm.
A multi-tool with thirty-two functions. Two of them work correctly. The rest are close.
A hand-held signal booster that extends the range of any comm unit it's connected to. Adds visible interference to the signal.
A combat knife with a mono-edge that holds its sharpness indefinitely. The handle is worn smooth.
A deployable decoy emitter that mimics a ship's drive signature on radar. Effective for thirty minutes before power drain.
A rebreather mask that recycles exhaled air and extends breathable capacity by six hours. Uncomfortable after the first two.
A shoulder-mounted light array originally designed for deep-sea work. Functions identically in vacuum.
A portable power cell that can recharge any standard device. Has a 23% charge remaining.
A field surgery kit with nanomed injectors. Each injector is single-use. Three remain.
A stealth coating in spray form — applies an anti-radar layer to any surface. Wears off in forty-eight hours.
A void rifle with a collapsed stock, chambered for armor-piercing rounds. Three rounds in the magazine.
A handheld EMP device with a fifteen-meter radius. Disables the user's own implants as well.
A compact sensor scrambler that makes the user invisible to standard security scans. Does not work on biological scans.
A set of atmospheric processing filters, rated for toxic environments. Missing one of four components.
A remote detonator with four channels. No charges are attached to it. The previous owner's channels are still programmed.
A high-density data card with storage capacity for an entire station's records. It has been used. It has not been wiped.
A magnetic grapple for attaching to hull surfaces from a distance. Rated for up to four hundred kilograms.
A pair of enhanced-vision goggles with light amplification, thermal, and narrow-band EM detection modes.
A gravity compensator worn at the belt that normalizes the user's perception in high-g environments up to 3G.
A deployable communication relay — drops in place and extends comm range across a dead zone for up to seventy-two hours.
A biometric spoofer worn on the wrist. Pre-loaded with one identity. The identity belongs to someone.
A hardened case with foam cutouts for equipment that isn't present. The cutouts indicate what was there.
A microfusion torch — burns hot enough to cut through a standard bulkhead in forty seconds. Two minutes of fuel remain.
A set of three signal flares rated for visibility in atmosphere and vacuum. One has been used.
A holographic projector the size of a coin. Projects a single stored image. The image is a face.
A tether gun that fires an adhesive anchor and a monofilament line. Rated for rescue operations.
A personal radiation counter that reads dose accumulation over time. It shows previous exposure that belongs to someone else.
A liquid-cooled combat vest rated for high-temperature environments. The cooling system is offline.
A portable water recycler that processes up to four liters per hour from any liquid source, including urine.
An acoustic dampener — worn around the neck — that silences the user's footsteps and movement entirely within three meters.
A compact defibrillator with integrated diagnostic AI. The AI offers commentary that most medical professionals find unnecessary.
A wrist-mounted map projector loaded with charts for a sector the party isn't in. Detailed, accurate, and wrong location.
A memory wipe device rated for use on unshielded electronic memory. Has been fired twice. The target logs are cleared.
A personal oxygen tank — four hours of supply — in a compact harness designed to fit under a jacket.
A shaped explosive charge designed to blow a door hinge without damaging what's behind the door. Two charges.
A cartridge-fed syringe loaded with a compound that forces consciousness on sedated subjects within thirty seconds.
A tracking tag, passive, the size of a fingernail. Requires a dedicated reader to detect. It was in someone's pocket.
A set of climbing anchors rated for use on metal, rock, and composite surfaces. Reusable.
A field-grade encryption module that can be connected to any standard comm. Renders transmissions unreadable without the paired key.
A compressed gas jet pack with ninety seconds of thrust. Intended for short-range repositioning in vacuum.
A sonic disruptor — fires a focused pressure wave that disorients but does not kill. Effective up to eight meters.
A sensor ghost emitter that creates a false biological reading at a set distance, drawing sensor attention away from the user.
A military-issue medpatch that accelerates wound closure and dulls pain. One dose. Significant side effects above three doses per week.
A folding breaching charge that adheres to a surface and creates a human-sized opening in any standard bulkhead material.
A small orbital map projector showing real-time positions of bodies in the current system. The data is forty minutes old.
A powered exo-brace for one arm that doubles grip strength and reduces fatigue during heavy labor.
A compact laser carbine with a folding stock, good range, low stopping power. Silenced. Illegal on twelve stations.
A deployable sensor node that passively monitors all electromagnetic traffic in a one-hundred-meter radius for up to a week.
A biological hazard detector that identifies airborne contagions, toxins, and pheromone-based compounds.
A smartlock bypass tool that works on any lock using the common commercial protocol. Does not work on military locks.
A modular survival pack with components for three different environment types. The user has removed one environment's components.
A wrist-mounted dart launcher with six darts. The darts are loaded with a compound that has no standard medical classification.
A data recovery chip that can extract information from damaged or partially destroyed electronic storage. One-time use.
A powered cutting torch with an articulated head for working in confined spaces. Full tank.
A set of three signal repeaters for extending comm coverage through shielded environments. Used by mining crews.
A combat shotgun loaded with gel rounds — non-lethal, but the gel expands on impact. Effective at close range.
A fold-flat stretcher rated for medical transport in vacuum. Stiff enough for spinal injuries. Compact enough to pocket.
A portable power inverter that can convert any energy source — including ship reactors — into a usable output for field equipment.
A holographic disguise projector that overlays a different face and body profile. Fools visual sensors. Fools nothing else.
A pressure-sensitive mat — two meters by one — that triggers a silent alarm when weight is placed on it. Set up, not yet triggered.
A nano-filament wire dispenser that can spin monofilament across a passage as a tripwire or restraint.
A hardened external drive containing a complete navigation database for six sectors. Encrypted. The encryption is solvable.
An atmospheric processor designed to convert an unbreathable atmosphere in a sealed space. Requires four hours and a power source.
A single-use emergency jump transponder that, when activated, broadcasts the user's location to every jump-capable vessel in the system.
A military-grade personal armor suit — compact, lightweight, rated for small arms fire. Missing the helmet.
A biomonitor implant — already installed in whoever is carrying this — that has been transmitting health data to an unknown receiver.
A locked case requiring a biometric key. The case is heavy. It hums faintly. The key is not present.
